This material is for the personal use of a delegate attending a course presented by BSI.No part of the materials may be reproduced, stored electronically, or transmitted in any form or by any means without the prior written consent of BSI.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

Please observe the following key points:For your personal safety, please be aware of the emergency exits from your classroom and the building.The tutor will inform you of the nearest restrooms.Please do not leave valuable items unattended in the classroom. Keep them with you or make other arrangements for their safekeeping.Please be considerate of other delegates, and avoid distractions from the beeping/flashing of your mobile phone.Please do not use recording devices since they may restrict free discussion.The tutor will inform you of the lunch and break schedule. Please return to class on time.The tutor will inform delegates of any area(s) known to be available for smoking.If there are any special needs, please confirm these now.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

The tutor will explain the outline or flow of the course.This course consists of tutorials, delegate activities and discussions.Your delegate workbook contains all materials related to this course, including slides, activities and reference materials. Sample answers to the activities are also in references document. Please only refer to the answers for each activity after completing it, or if you’re really stuck.You will get the most out of this course by being an active participant, asking questions and engaging in discussions and activities. Remember there are no silly questions and please feel free to seek the help of your tutor as needed.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Evaluating frameworks, standards, and codes of conduct

Two short breaks will be taken at suitably convenient times in the morning and afternoon. 45 minutes will be given for a lunch break. Additional breaks may be taken as long as agreed by delegates and tutor, and all learning objectives are met. Course activities will be at the tutor’s discretion, depending on time and delegate needs. Finish times may therefore differ to those advertised.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Upon completion of this course, you will be able to:
Explain the types of security threat to and from built assets and their information
Recognize security issues related to ISO 19650, Common Data Environment (CDE) and BIM
Explain the documentation and execution of policies required for security-mindedness
Define the role of security manager

Course aimTo give those tasked with managing collaborative working and BIM projects/asset management the core knowledge and skills required to understand and implement security management.Upon completion of this course, you will be able to: Explain the types of security threat to and from built assets and their informationRecognize security issues related to ISO 19650, Common Data Environment (CDE) and BIMDefine the role of security managerExplain the documentation and execution of policies required for security-mindedness
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

The structure of this course broadly follows the structure of the ISO itself but brings in the annexes where relevant. The first session is concerned with the overview of security considerations in relation to built assets and project/asset information. This includes looking at some definitions and some informative ideas about what should be safeguarded, and against what threats. The second session looks at the strategic considerations for security-minded asset and information management and how the security strategy needs to be formulated. The requirements for this are contained in Clauses 4, 5 and 6.The third session looks at management issues – how security-mindedness is managed at project or asset level. This involves turning the strategy into a management plan, including how security breaches are to be dealt with. The requirements here are in Clauses 7 and 8, and the types of controls in Annex B. The final session looks at operational issues – how security-mindedness is implemented at project or asset level. This involves turning the management plan into specific controls, including assessing third-parties and establishing formal agreement, as outlined in Clause 9, Annexs, C and D, so that you start planning your next steps in relation to implementing security-mindedness.OverviewForeword, introduction and 1. Scope2. Normative references3. Terms and definitionsStrategy4. Need5. Approach6. StrategyManagement7. Management8. Breach managementImplementation9. Working with appointed partiesC. Assessment of third-partiesD. Information sharing agreements
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Overview of ISO 19650 and security risks

Security-mindedness is an overarching concept for the built environment – it does not just apply where ISO 19650 is being implemented, but is needed across all projects and in relation to all built assets.However, ‘Collaborative BIM’ brings some particular challenges in relation to security of information, and ISO 19650-5 is part of the suite of standards developed in support this.Some issues, in particular relating to electronic information, may equally arise with any BIM implementation whether simpler or more advanced. This session will explore the different nature of security risks, both physical security and cyber security, and the relationship between them. It will also explain how security-mindedness is most helpfully viewed as an exercise in risk management, and where it has parallels with other pervading principles, such as health and safety.This section will cover:Security risks in relation to built assets and informationImpact of Collaborative BIM on cyber securityTerms (3) Security context (Appendix A)
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
ISO 19650-5: Security-minded approach to information management
ISO 19650Part 6 Health and safety information

ISO 19650-5 Security-minded approach to information management has been developed as part of the ISO 19650 suite of standards and tools. As such it is clearly aimed at those implementing ‘Collaborative BIM’ on their projects (ISO 19650-2) and in the management of their assets (ISO 19650-3).ISO 19650-5 has a much wider application than just ‘Collaborative BIM’. Indeed, a conscious approach to security in relation to built assets and the information about them should be included in all project and asset management activities. In that sense ISO 19650-5 would apply across the whole range of the BIM implementation. Although the challenges of managing security in an environment where information is not systematically managed would be huge.ISO 27001 covers security within a single organization and should be implemented alongside ISO 19650-5.UK: In the UK, the security-minded approach is promoted National Protective Security Authority (NPSA) The NPSA is the latest UK government authority for protective security advice to the UK national infrastructure. Its role is to protect national security by helping to reduce the vulnerability of the national infrastructure to terrorism and other threats. At present no guidance relating to BIM and ISO 19650 is available and so included within your handouts are documents from the former government authority the Centre for Protection of National Infrastructure (CPNI) for your references.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

A key point in ISO 19650-5 is that security-mindedness must run through a client organization and all the collaborative activity involved in projects and asset management. Experience and lessons-learned must flow back to maintain the appropriate level of security-mindedness.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Information quality and validitySystem configuration
Continuity of operations Safety of personnel and assets

Security is a concern for both physical and information assets. The threats take many forms, including physical intrusion for theft of valuable goods (safe deposit), or to cause damage by vehicles, or to gain unauthorized access (security barriers). Cyber threats also involve unauthorized access (to damage computer systems) or theft of digital information. Theft can be overt (a physical intruder copying information from a computer) or covert (interception of data/information during its transit from one location to another).Bear in mind that cyber security breaches may give access to the physical asset, and physical security breaches may give access to data. This is one of the reasons why the threats to built assets themselves and the information they involve need to be considered in a holistic way.It is important to understand the basics of security and why the concept of security forms a critical part of ‘Collaborative BIM’. This diagram organizes the concepts in ISO 19650-5 Annex B.3.3.Confidentiality – The control of access and prevention of unauthorized access to information which may be sensitive in isolation or in aggregate.Possession - Ensure that the prevention of unauthorized control, manipulation or interference is maintainedIntegrity - Maintain the consistency, coherence and configuration of information and systems, and preventing unauthorized changes to themUtility - That asset information and systems remain usable and useful across the lifecycle of a built assetAuthenticity - Ensuring that inputs to and from built asset systems are genuine and have not been tampered withAvailability - To ensure that asset information is accessible and reliableSafety - Prevention of harmful states which may lead to injury or loss of life in regard to the design, implementation, operation and maintenance of the built asset systemResilience - The ability of the asset information and systems to transform, renew and recover in a timely way in response to adverse events
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
ISO 19650 Part 5: Security-minded approach to information management

NeedApproachStrategyManagementBreach managementWorking with appointed parties
Security contextTypes of controlsAssessment of third-partiesInformation sharing agreements

This presents the structure of ISO 19650-5 (and of most ISO standards). The document has introductory clauses including terms and definitions. Clause 4 onwards contains the ‘normative’ requirements, and the annexes provide ‘informative’ supportive information.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
State of relative freedom from threat or harm caused by deliberate, unwanted, hostile or malicious acts.
Potential cause of an incident which may result in harm.

Understanding and routinely applying appropriate and proportionate security (3.7) measures in any business situation so as to deter and/or disrupt hostile, malicious, fraudulent and criminal behaviours or activities.
‘Security-minded’ is defined in Clause 3.10, but this refers to another entry for ‘security’ which refers to another entry for ‘threat’.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

Security threats come in a number of different guises. ISO 19650-5 is not intended to be definitive on all the different possible threats, but it does highlight the most significant issues.Malicious acts can come from a variety of sources – installed malware, breaches by external individuals, breaches caused by or aided by internal staff or contractors.Release of personal data is now covered by much more stringent requirements through the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) which came into force in May 2018. Other relevant regulations include those concerned with environmental protection and freedom of information requests.Loss or disclosure of intellectual property can include a range of types of material, including designs, trade secrets, proprietary processes, and physical prototypes.Loss or disclosure of commercially sensitive material can include financial details, especially around pricing of goods and services. In a commercial environment it is necessary that some pricing information is transferred to the prospective client organization – the supplier has to have confidence that the client can and will keep that information secure.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

The value and longevity of initiatives and projectsThe value, longevity and ongoing use of the organization’s assets, products and servicesPersonnel or other users of the asset and servicesInformation and systemsPersonal data, intellectual property and commercially sensitive informationReputation (against risks from the above items)
Terrorism Hostile actions by countries Commercial espionage Organized crimeActivists Lone actors Hackers Malicious insiders

Understanding potential security issues involves recognizing both the assets and information that need safeguarding and identifying the sources of potential threats. It is essential to evaluate the types of assets or information at risk from security threats, as well as the reputation risks that could arise from these issues. Security threats come from a wide range of sources, including highly planned and sophisticated organizations, as well as opportunistic individuals. However, not all these sources will be relevant to every asset owner or project client, making a tailored approach to security vital.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

Aggregation risks arise from compromised information:Accumulation, where the volume of information stored together increases the level of impactAssociation, where the association of different types of information, which in themselves have little or no impact, have a higher level of impact when associated

Hostile reconnaissance seeks reliable information about how the security system works in order to exploit it:Security (e.g. physical vulnerabilities or system configuration)The modus operandi and the pattern-of-life of an individual, group, or of an assetThe state of security (i.e. the chances of being undetected or successful)

Hostile reconnaissance can be instigated to identify any potential weaknesses in physical or information systems that can be exploited. This reconnaissance can itself be a combination of physical activities (such as watching a facility to spot patterns in comings or goings or to identify weaknesses in physical protection or controls) and cyber activities (phishing exercises to trick people into revealing passwords or installing malware on their device). Hostile reconnaissance is usually seen as the necessary pre-cursor to the planned security breach. This topic is covered in ISO 19650-5 Annex A.1 b) Note 2.It is also important to understand the potential for the aggregation of information to:Lead to the identification of individuals or groups of individualsReveal sensitive information about initiatives, projects, assets, products, services, individuals or communitiesReveal information about the configuration of assets, products, components and/or software in a systemThis topic is covered in ISO 19650-5 Annex A.1 g) Note 5.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Threats to physical assets similar to those in the past
Threats to information assets are expanding

How have threats changed over recent years?The physical threats to built assets are pretty much the same as they have been in the past. Buildings and infrastructure need to be protected from unauthorized access (for all sorts of reasons, including prevention of damage). Buildings in particular may have valuable contents that need to be protected and the building itself is one of the means of doing this. The motives for these actions have always been a mixture of the political/ideological, the commercial and the personal.The threats to information assets are much more widespread than they have been in the past, primarily because of the exponential increase in digital information and data being generated and used. The increasing numbers of organizations and people gathering, using, transmitting digital information concerning projects and asset management gives rise to so many more opportunities for security to be compromised. Allied to this is the increasingly central position of digital information, in commercial transactions or providing public services, so it is becoming more and more important to the smooth running of everyday life.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Activity 1: What are the physical and cyber security risks for a city centre HQ building?
Discuss and identify the range of physical and cyber security risks associated with a headquarters building in a city centre.
Use the information provided in the previous slides and ISO 19650-5 Annex A as a reference to guide your discussion.

Activity 1: What are the physical and cyber security risks for a city centre HQ building? Purpose: To explore the nature and range of security risks for a particular type of built asset and its asset/project informationDuration:20 minutes10 minutes feedbackDirections:Think about the types of security risk that would need to be considered in relation to a headquarters building in a city centre. Decide whether you are concerned with the design and construction project to create the building, or the long-term operation and maintenance of the building.Use the previous slides and ISO 19650-5 Annex A.Be prepared to share your results with the rest of the group.The tutor will then introduce a list of suggestions from your References section. You should only refer to these at the completion of this activity.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

This section looks at the high-level, strategic considerations for managing security around built asset information.The first part of this section builds up to the security triage process – an approach for each asset owner or project client to apply to their asset/project situation. A key question in the triage process is to determine whether the built asset is “sensitive” using a range of criteria. The outcome of the triage is a conclusion on the level of security management that is needed, and this guides the application of the remainder of the ISO 19650-5 requirements.The second part of the session looks at the development and review of the security strategy.Establishing the need (4)Initiating the security-minded approach (5)Developing a security strategy (6)
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Review when starting …ProjectManagement of an assetSupply of a service
Revisit when change to …ContextProject/asset, Service or productInformation held

Security needs to be kept under review. This does not mean that the policy, strategy and procedures are re-examined continuously, but there are some significant changes during the asset lifecycle where the security-minded approach needs to be reviewed.These include times when the use of the asset changes significantly, for example where a factory starts making a new product that has a much higher security profile, or when the occupier of the asset changes, such as when a government security agency moves into a building previously occupied by a corporate administration department.Security triggers related to the asset also include when major work is carried out on the asset, such as remodelling floors, extending or altering the building. This kind of trigger is particularly important where infrastructure is concerned, since in these cases the security related to personnel is likely to be covered elsewhere.Finally, triggers for security review also include the kinds of work that particularly relate to security weak-points. For example, updating the Building Management System may involve new internet connections with all the risks for hacking and unauthorized IT access. Or changing a maintenance contractor will involve a new group of individuals needing to be given access to various parts of the asset to carry out their duties.Security should be reviewed when major event occur such as:Realization that an existing physical or information asset is at stakeWhen a project is initiatedWhen information is being collected
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Security context or the built asset has a change of the:
UseOccupierRemodellingExtension and alterationBMS (Building Management System) or control systemsMaintenance contract
Or a change to the context, in terms of:
PoliticalEconomicSocialTechnologicalThreat environmentSensitivity
The built asset security strategy needs to be reviewed at regular intervals. As before, this needs to happen when:

The built asset security strategy also needs reviewing at each project milestone.The rationale for this is that the nature of the risk and the completeness of the information becomes clearer as the project progresses. There are also developments in the structure of the team, especially in the transition from consultant-led design to contractor-led installation. Depending on the procurement route of the project, this may happen earlier or later during design development.The nature of the design and the procurement process means that there are major information exchanges during the project and these clearly give rise to risks for cyber security to be compromised.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
InitiativeProject AssetProductService, as well as Any associated information
How should both the organization and the threat be considered together in terms of sensitivity?

‘Considered sensitive’ is a key phrase , ‘sensitivity’ suggests that it both the organization and the threat need to be considered together.This question tries to show the range of what may be sensitive, that it may just be part, not necessarily the whole. The differences between existing (in-use) assets and new (planned) assets is of course important but the two are locked in a single lifecycle.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

Whether or not an asset is sensitive from a security point of view is a key criterion for how many of ISO 19650-5 requirements have to be applied in any given case.‘Sensitive built asset’ is explained in clauses.

Clause 4.3.2:Critical national infrastructureDefence, law-enforcement, national security or diplomaticCommercial site involving valuable materialsLandmark, nationally significant site or crowded placeEvents of security significanceClause 4.3.3:Compromise the functioning, integrity, safety, security and/or resilience of the wholeClause 4.3.4:Excessive risk to safety, security and/privacy of individuals or communitiesSometimes it may apply to only part of an asset, such as:Specific systems or productsSpecific levels or zonesSpecific events or processes in a project
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Will access be, or has already been, gained to sensitive information about another organization, its asset, products or services that is not otherwise publicly available?
Triage is the procedure of assigning levels of priority to tasks to determine the most effective order in which to deal with them
Is the asset owner's or clients own asset, project or information considered sensitive?
*Protect sensitive information Protect commercial and personal information

Understanding the security threat to an asset is important as it will allow a strategy to be put in place in order to manage it. It is recommended that the owner would set in place a process to ensure the security-minded approach of the building has been considered. This would be undertaken by the security triage process. The above diagram illustrates the security triage to identify the need for the security-minded approach to the asset or any asset information. Once this process has been worked through with the relevant authorities that can support the decision-making process, the following can be extrapolated:Is the asset sensitive in terms of security and if so, what measures need to be put in place?Would there be a requirement to manage the asset information to neighbouring built assets?Is there a need for more than baseline security measures and if so, what needs to be put into place to achieve this?
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

Sensitive information is defined in Clause 3.11. It is always worth reviewing Clause 3 in any standard as this is where terms and definitions are found. Also look out for the Normative References listed in Clause 2 as it will be assumed you are familiar with any definitions in these standards as well.3.11 - Sensitive informationInformation the loss, misuse or modification or unauthorized access to, can:Adversely affect the privacy, security or safety of an individual or individuals;Compromise intellectual property or trade secrets of an organization;Cause commercial or economic harm to an organization or country; and/orJeopardize the security, internal and foreign affairs of a nation4.10 - In all cases:Commercial and contractual informationPersonal and privacy information
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Built assets in the neighbourhood of the built asset under consideration.

An example of ‘another organization’ could be built assets in the neighbourhood of the built asset under consideration:That share a boundary (including beneath it or overhead)That are physically separatedBy a public or private streetPublic or privately owned open spaceOr similar featureAlso, the ‘catchment area’ for security-mindedness around an assetThe radius within which security is of interest will depend on the nature of either or both assetsOne specific concern is the leakage of information relating to a neighbouring built asset. This is particularly the case with infrastructure but will also apply to buildings.Information about a neighbouring asset might be inadvertently gathered, for example through project surveys for utility connections, access or site layout, and this might be shared unwittingly within the project team. There may also be inadvertent publication on documents such as planning applications.It is important to remember that asset boundaries may be horizontal or vertical or span public space. In the latter case, there may not be any specific neighbouring ‘assets’ but the security catchment area might extend a significant distance beyond any physical fence-line or asset boundary. For example, discussions on the drone-exclusion zone around airports, or the security zone around military facilities such as firing ranges or practice grounds.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Individual accountable at top management level.
Developing or implementing the security strategy
Cascading to third-parties and supply chain
Embedding into procurement and appointment documents

Accountability within the asset owner or project client. Ultimate accountability has to remain with the asset owner/operator, or with the project client. Responsibility for various security activities might be delegated outside of the owner/client.Responsibility for specific policies might be delegated; this might be inside owner/client or into supply chain. Large projects or complex assets might benefit from a security committee.UK: Guidance on procuring contract personnel in a security-minded way is available from the CPNI.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

The high-level strategy for managing security risk is the same for other areas of risk management. Within the framework of a risk management strategy, risks are assessed, then mitigated, then residual risks are controlled and the whole process is regularly reviewed. The cycle includes:AssessmentIdentify sensitive assets and informationAssess the value or impact of loss of the asset/informationAssess the threats and vulnerabilities to the asset/informationAssess the likelihood of the threatMitigationAlso refer to Annex B which introduces types of controls to be considered: Personnel, Physical security, Technological considerations (including selection of software), Information securityAcceptanceAcknowledgement that some residual risks exist and that control measures need to be implemented for theseReviewKeeping the security strategy under review, then repeating the cycleMitigation of risks involves an initial review of whether the impacts are acceptable, and if not then mitigation measures to reduce the impact of loss, the vulnerabilities of the asset/information or the likelihood of the threat.The process of assessment and mitigation is iterative until an acceptable position has been reached within the constraints of time and cost available.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Generally, the asset owner should identify and develop a security strategy which should include:
Security strategy (Clauses 1 and 6):Appropriate, proportionate, mindset and culture (Clause 1)Security strategy is itself a security risk (Clause 4.5)
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
The security requirements as outlined previously:SocietalCommercial and Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)EnvironmentalPolitical
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
A risk management strategy:Risk identificationThreats and vulnerabilitiesPeopleProcessPhysical assetTechnologicalNature of harm
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Mitigations and measures:CostEffectivenessOther effectsNew vulnerabilitiesResidual risks
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Communication and Coordination:A list of the individuals to be informed regarding riskControlImplications for supply chainThe mechanism for reviewing and updating the strategy
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

It is recommended that access to the security strategy will be restricted and only those closely related to the asset management system and details will be allowed access. The strategy forms an important part of the overall information requirements to be managed and reviewed on the built asset. This requirement to ensure a strategy is created and assessed forms a large part of the process toward managing an asset with the adequate processes in place to be able to assure security on the given asset.UK: There is a security strategy template from Centre for the Protection of National Infrastructure (CPNI) included in the Reference materials (file 03.2) – this is quite complex so could be simplified if the asset owner/project client thought that was appropriate.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Activity 2: What is a sensitive asset?
Explore and discuss the concept of sensitivity in the context of assets, facilities, and information.
Discuss the extent to which information about these assets is publicly accessible or known.
The activity will be followed by a group discussion.
A useful first step is to identify what is a sensitive asset?
How can information be released inadvertently?

Activity 2: What is a sensitive asset?Purpose: To discuss ‘sensitivity’ in practice.Duration:15 minutes15 minutes feedbackDirections:Identify examples from different categories of facility and threat Think, how much information is public?Elements to think about:  How can information be released inadvertently? How can this be minimized?Note your answers ahead of a group discussion with the tutor, example answers can be found in your references section.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
A useful first step is to identify what is a sensitive asset?
How can information be released inadvertently?
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

This section will explore the management of security-mindedness on a project or in connection with an operating asset. This connects to Clauses 7 and 8 and Annex B in ISO 19650-5:Security management plan (7) Types of controls (Annex B)Incident management plan (8)Some of the structures and processes in ISO 19650-5 mirror those in other parts of the ISO 19650 series. In particular, the need for a day-to-day management plan to set out processes and procedures reflects the concept of an information delivery cycle with information requirements in ISO 19650-2 and 3.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

The activity defined in ISO 19650-5 is for Security Management. It is important to remember that this is a role, not a job-title, so it is not necessarily the case that a specific member of staff will have to be appointed to carry out this role. Of course, on a large, complex project or asset, or across a portfolio of projects or assets, it may be appropriate to have a dedicated resource or team to provide leadership for this work.Like health and safety and many other “management” functions, security is really everybody's responsibility, and organizations should take care that if they do appoint a specific resource this does not entitle everyone else to believe that this issue is no longer their concern.The principal task of the security manager is to act for the asset owner or employer, depending on whether ISO 19650-5 is being applied to asset management of an existing asset, or to design and construction of a new asset.The three main aspects to the role are as follows:Guidance and direction on the handling of security-related risksPromoting a security-minded culture throughoutSecurity issues and threatsHandling of risksTake ownership and management responsibility for the implementation of the security strategy (next slide) and the security/incident management plans and information requirementsSecurity strategyBreach/incident management planSecurity information requirementsSupporting documentation, policies, process and proceduresSupport the implementation of key processes, contributing to the PIR/AIR, tendering, project planning etc.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

The Security Management Plan will be informed by the security strategy and contain the following information: Policies which set out the security strategyProcedures which describe the detailed work and instructions for implementation and operation delivery of the processes of the security strategyProcesses which are derived from security policies through the life of the assetMethods for dealing with shared informationPrecautions to be taken in storage and protection of informationHow any breach of security or similar incident is to be handledHow security-mindedness is to be cascaded to suppliers through contractual arrangementsThird-party relationships such as with government agencies and the incident plan are considered in detail later.Logistical security would apply to any equipment that is security sensitive and could be compromised before installation and commissioning.The details in the Security Management Plan would then feed through into project and asset specific information requirements and information management resources (such as the information standard or information production methods and procedures).
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Cloud-based web accessible databases, processing and filing system

When considering CDEs and BIM, and electronic media generally:Information is held on local drives/servers/cloud-servicesInformation is exchanged (transmitted) usually over public utilitiesThe onion-rings show how different storage media attract increasing cyber risks.The nature of the risk is also the nature of the benefit:More information may be available to give a more complete pictureInformation in combination may be more revealingThe information may be more accurate The information may be subject to analysisInformation that is naturally hidden in the physical world, such as utilities, may be visible in the cyber realmIncreasing ease of access and number of users increases the potential for a security breach or incident:Access to repositories should be managed and controlled to minimize the chances for unauthorized personnel to access informationInformation exchanges (transfers of information from one organization to another) should be encrypted. This needs to apply whether the transfer takes place via physical media (e.g. USB sticks) or electronically (e.g. uploading and downloading data via a https:// site rather than a http:// site)Specific precautions should be taken in relation to personal information. This is particularly important within the EU as the new General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) includes severe financial penalties for mismanagement of personal information
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
All management plans need to be monitored/audited to confirm:
Security management plan reviews might lead to changes to scope in contracts.

Reviews of the security management plan need to respond to the results of monitoring/auditing, and external factors, such as changes in legislation.Security management plans are like any other management plan, in that they need regular monitoring to check that they are meeting their objectives. They might from time to time need to be updated.Updates might be as a result of monitoring implementation or might be due to external changes.External changes could relate to regulations, or changes in an overarching policy. External changes will need to be spotted by the project client or asset owner/operator, so some on-going horizon-scanning will be needed to do this.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Identify high risk functions, roles and activities
Access and permission to information and information models
Induction for new personnel and organizations

Personnel is one of the four considerations in drawing up policies, processes and procedures within the security management plan.Considerations relating to personnel include:Working out which positions in the organization are high-risk – this includes positions in related organizations such as contractors and suppliersSetting the appropriate screening and vetting requirements – both project/asset wide and for particular roles UK: BS 7858 Security screening of individuals employed in a security environment might be helpfulTraining requirements to support security-mindedness – this might include attending courses such as this oneInduction of new personnel - some of this would be project/asset-specific training and some would be general principles such as good cyber-hygieneTraining and access requirements to the project/asset information – how the rules of the CDE work and will be enforcedDemobilization of personnel either when they leave the organization/project or when the project comes to an end
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Neighbouring and adjacency securityProtective measures for:Computing EquipmentDevices
Control of confidentiality and possessionOffice and site security at the level of:Facility ZoneSpace

Physical aspects of security-mindedness is the second perspective to be considered during the management plan.Physical considerations include:Physical security of the asset itself (such as access control for people, protection from physical attack by vehicles, projectiles or missiles) and of locations being used in the design, construction or manufacture of components for the projectProtection of neighboring assetsProtective measures around the use of computers and other electronic devices at the asset (e.g. cameras in phones/tablets)Protective measures around the provision of the Common Data Environment (CDE) for the asset or project information (e.g. secure location and physical measures to restrict access)
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Configuration to full security when in use

Technical aspects of security-mindedness is the third perspective to be considered during the management plan.Technical considerations include:Cyber security measures for the data and information systems (e.g. firewalls, encryption). This might include using “friendly hacker” tests to check the effectiveness of control measures in place.Cyber security measures for hardware, especially portable hardware (e.g. no USB connectivity, no disc drives, user passwords)Appropriate levels of software trustworthinessMethods to delete/destroy asset or project information held by organizations who leave the team and to rescind passwords and system access – demobilizationMethods to deal with the longevity of information (especially asset information) and the need to update or upgrade software and hardware periodically during this time
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Applying across all phases of the information lifecycle
Protection against loss, copying or corruption
Surveys/ inspections that might gather sensitive information

Information aspects of security-mindedness is the fourth perspective to be considered during the security management plan.The key themes remain control of confidentiality and availability.Consider what information will be held as a maximum. At the start of a project or in-use cycle, the information held may seem trivial and reproduce information held elsewhere. Progressively the information will be more complete, more specific and more valuableConsider if information can or should be destroyed, or moved to a secure off-line repository
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Explore security considerations for a built asset or project relevant to your organization and identify key topics for a comprehensive security management plan.
Discuss the chosen asset or project from the perspective of the security considerations that should be included in a management plan.

Activity 3: Practical implementation of security-mindedness Purpose: To consider a built asset or project that is relevant to your organization or type of organization, and practice identifying topics to be covered in a security management plan.Duration:15 minutes15 minutes feedbackDirections:Select an asset-type or project scenario that your group can relate to. Then discuss the asset/project scenario from the perspective of the security considerations that should be included in the management plan. Elements to think about: The four perspectives of the management plan (personnel, physical, technological and information)The implications of cloud servicesThe balance/mix between physical security risks and cyber security risks Following the sub-group discussions, the tutor will invite feed back to the whole group. A sample answer is provided in the References section of your course pack. Do not look at this until you have attempted the activity.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Step 1: Register of potential risks from a breach/incident
Step 2:  Processes to be applied following a breach/incident
Step 4:  Business continuity and recovery actions

It is to be hoped that security breaches and incidents will not occur, but this cannot be relied upon. A breach management plan needs to be drawn up, ready for when any breach or incident takes place.ISO 19650-5 includes some headline topics that need to be included in such a plan. This has to cover the foreseeable range of potential physical and cyber breaches/incidents. The plan should also provide a good starting point for action if an unforeseen breach/incident takes place, as this has to be responded to and might also be included in an update version of the plan.Processes to be applied following a breach/incident will include:Who to contact immediately on discovery of the breach/incident and how to contact them (their details)How to work out what has been lost, damaged or compromisedHow to work out who any concerned parties are (such as individuals whose personal data may have been compromised, or individuals who may have been put at more risk because of the incident)How to liaise with any relevant third-parties (such as a regulator, the media)How to liaise with the general public (if it is decided that this is appropriate)
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Activity 4: Responses to a security breach
Analyze a hypothetical security breach related to your previously discussed asset or project and outline necessary mitigating measures for a breach/incident management plan.
Consider how the CDE, conformity and continuity is managed.

Activity 4: Responses to a security breachPurpose: To consider a hypothetical breach (built asset and/or project) and practice identifying mitigating measures that would need to be covered in the breach/incident management plan.Duration:15 minutes work15 minutes feedbackDirections:Using the information from Activity 3, identify a security breach or incident related to the asset or project discussed for Activity 3. Discuss what steps would need to be taken to respond to the breach/incident, and would need to be included in the breach management plan.Elements to think about: What needs to happen immediately (within a few hours) after the breach has been discovered?What needs to happen in the short term (within a few days)?What needs to happen in the medium and longer term?Think about who needs to be involved with each mitigating action. What types of permanent measures could be put in place to avoid a similar occurrence in the future?A sample answer is provided in the References section of your course pack. Do not look at this until you have attempted the activity.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

This final section of the course focuses on implementation, with some detail on agreements and is also an opportunity to review the key messages and requirements from ISO 19650-5 about implementing security-mindedness in BIM projects and asset management. It is also the opportunity for you to reflect on the aspects of security-mindedness that are most relevant to your organization and to start identifying what your organization’s next steps need to be.This section will cover: Working with appointed parties (9)Working with third-parties (Annex C)Information sharing agreements (Annex D)
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

Security-mindedness is required in the relationship between a project client or asset owner/operator and any suppliers appointed to carry out work on or in relation to the asset/project.This security-mindedness needs to influence the procurement activity, the contract that is put in place, the work of the supplier during the contract and the exit period at the end of the contract.This is covered in ISO 19650-5 Clause 9. Some key considerations are:Making sure that suppliers agree to act in a security-minded way (and know the appropriate procedures) before access is given to any sensitive information during procurement/ tenderingMaking sure that the unsuccessful bidders for the work take the necessary steps to return/destroy/delete relevant data and information they were given access toPutting appropriate clauses in the contract, such as the right of the employer to inspect a supplier’s security arrangements and compliance with the requirementsCheck that security-mindedness is appropriately covered in the asset or project information standard and/or the asset or project information production methods and proceduresCheck that project and asset delivery teams have appropriate resources (roles) and understand their responsibilitiesMaking sure that sensitive data and information is deleted/destroyed or securely stored at the end of the contract, as part of a formal de-mobilization processOne area where security-mindedness can affect the management of design is through the federation strategy to define “restricted areas” in the information model on the basis of information security clearance. This would mean that only selected members of the project or asset team would have the necessary permission to see information in a particular part of the information model.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

Security needs to be established before there is an appointment and in particular during the tender/invitation process. An information sharing agreement should be in place before any sensitive information or the possibility that there may be sensitive information, is shared.This needs to cover appropriate handling and destruction. So, part of the invitation/tender process must be an assessment of their capability.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Right to require and verify destruction assessment

The contractual cascade must ensure that suppliers are bound by appropriate aspects of the security strategy. In addition, the client/owner may need the right to review and inspect the virtual and the physical aspects of the security in place. There may be a need to refine or adjust the strategy in the light of external changes. Finally there may need to be a right to require and verify the destruction of any information the appointment is completed. These expectations are of course expected to be ‘appropriate and proportionate’. Annex C gives some points on assessing third-parties. It is important that the implications of not sharing are considered.A specific difficulty may be dealing with regulatory and statutory processes which may imply a large degree of disclosure. Some countries have ‘Freedom of Information’ (FoI) legislation which may be part of a transparency or democratic approach. It may be necessary to separate and negotiate the scope of open information.Lastly public presentations may inadvertently add to the sum of information that is publicly available – especially where the project is of public interest, pride or innovation.UK: The UK BIM Framework templates for the Information Protocol include contractual clauses to incorporate all these considerations into a project or asset contract.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course
Information assessmentWho will have accessJustification for sharing
Public access to informationTransparency and FoI
Public presentations and publications Arrangements for permission
Regulatory and statutory processesMechanism for compliance and security

Annex C gives some points on assessing third-parties. It is important that the implications of not sharing are considered.A specific difficulty may be dealing with regulatory and statutory processes which may imply a large degree of disclosure. Some countries have ‘Freedom of Information’ (FoI) legislation which may be part of a transparency or democratic approach. It may be necessary to separate and negotiate the scope of open information.Lastly public presentations may inadvertently add to the sum of information that is publicly available – especially where the project is of public interest, pride or innovation.Information assessment An information assessment should identify who can access the shared information, whether stakeholders need to be consulted before sharing or publishing, and the specific reasons or justification for sharing or publishing the information. This helps ensure appropriate use and protection of data throughout the process. Public access to information The security management plan should outline how sensitive information will be protected when an organization receives a request under public access or transparency laws. It must also consider the risks associated with data aggregation and address potential impacts to ensure secure and responsible information handling Public presentations and publicationsThe security management plan should define approval requirements for any material about the initiative, project, asset, product, or service that will be shown at public events, displayed in public areas, shared with third parties, or published on websites, in academic or technical publications, or marketing materials. Regulatory and statutory processesThe security management plan should outline how to securely share information with third parties while meeting legal requirements. Sensitive data must be protected through methods like redaction, alternative formats, or prior agreements on safeguards—especially when dealing with public access laws, to ensure risks remain at an acceptable level for the organization.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

Annex D offers an outline for an information agreement (or annex to a main agreement).The agreement should define the agreed and permissible purposes for sharing and may exclude other purposes for using the information.The agreement then serves to cascade the main points of the security management plan and incident management plan.
ISO 19650 Part 5: Building Information Modelling (BIM) Security-mindedness Training Course

Activity 5: Action planning for BIM security-mindednessPurpose: To reflect on the concepts and ideas discussed during this course and consider how to move forward with these in your own organization.Duration:30 minutes Directions:Review the sessions of this course and complete the blank action plan overleaf, considering the following:The security context for your projects or assets (or the projects/assets you might be involved with in the future)The strategic considerations for asset and information securityThe operational considerations for asset and information securityThink about which areas your organization is most advanced in its journey towards Collaborative BIMHow can your organization maintain the level of implementation it already has?Think about the areas of BIM security-mindedness where your organization needs to make the biggest steps forwardWhat firm actions are needed to move forward in these areas?In your action plan, describe the objective to be achieved, the deadline for completion, how you would measure that it has been done, and what resources are needed to make it happen (people, training, equipment, buy-in from Directors etc.).As this action plan is private to you, then there is no need to discuss it with other organizations on the course. You probably won’t have time to finish your plan, so do as much as you can to sketch it out and then complete it later.Some ideas are included in your References section, but use these only as a prompt for your own thoughts and ideas.
